home.title: Physics Revision
home.subtitle: Grade 9 — Interactive Course
home.intro: Study topics, explore key terms, and experiment with interactive simulations. Everything you need for the MYP Physics 9 exam — in one calm, focused place.

forceMotion.title: Force & Motion
forceMotion.desc: Vectors, Newton's laws, momentum and braking.

forcesEnergy.title: Forces & Energy
forcesEnergy.desc: Hooke's law, pressure, density and energy transfer.

electricity.title: Electricity & Magnetism
electricity.desc: Ohm's law, circuits, sensors, power consumption.

wavesOptics.title: Waves & Optics
wavesOptics.desc: Waves, reflection, refraction, the EM spectrum.

commandTerms.title: Command Terms
commandTerms.intro: Command terms tell you exactly what an exam question is asking for. Read them carefully and answer accordingly.

state.meaning: Give a specific fact or piece of information.
state.how: Just give the fact. No explanation needed.
state.example: State the SI unit of force. → newton (N).

describe.meaning: Give a detailed account of what happens.
describe.how: Outline the events or features in order.
describe.example: Describe the motion of a parachutist before reaching terminal velocity.

explain.meaning: Give reasons for why or how something happens.
explain.how: Use 'because' and link cause to effect.
explain.example: Explain why a thicker wire has lower resistance.

compare.how: Use linking words: similarly, however, whereas.

evaluate.meaning: Weigh up strengths and weaknesses, then reach a judgement.
evaluate.example: Evaluate the use of nuclear power.

calculate.meaning: Work out a numerical answer using a formula.
calculate.how: Write the formula, substitute, calculate, give units.
calculate.example: Calculate the force on a 5 kg mass accelerating at 2 m/s².

forceMotion.title: Force and Motion
forceMotion.intro: We study how and why objects move, what makes them speed up or stop, and what happens when they collide.

scalar.name: Scalar
scalar.definition: A quantity with magnitude only and no direction.

vector.name: Vector
vector.definition: A quantity with both magnitude and direction.
vector.example: Velocity, force and displacement are vectors.

displacement.name: Displacement
displacement.definition: The shortest distance between the start and end point in a specific direction.
displacement.example: Walking 3 m east then 4 m north gives a displacement of 5 m NE.

acceleration.name: Acceleration
acceleration.definition: The rate of change of velocity over time.
acceleration.example: A car going from 0 to 20 m/s in 4 s accelerates at 5 m/s².

resultantForce.name: Resultant force
resultantForce.definition: The overall force acting on an object after all forces are combined.
resultantForce.example: 10 N right − 4 N left = 6 N right.

newton1.name: Newton's First Law
newton1.definition: Objects stay at rest or move uniformly unless acted on by a resultant force.
newton1.example: A book on a table stays still until pushed.

newton2.name: Newton's Second Law
newton2.definition: Acceleration depends on force and mass (F = m × a).
newton2.example: Doubling the force doubles the acceleration.

newton3.name: Newton's Third Law
newton3.definition: Every action force has an equal and opposite reaction force.
newton3.example: When you jump, you push down on Earth, Earth pushes you up.

momentum.name: Momentum
momentum.definition: The quantity of motion of an object (p = mv).
momentum.example: A heavy truck at low speed can have the same momentum as a light car at high speed.

impulse.name: Impulse
impulse.definition: The change in momentum caused by a force over time (J = FΔt).
impulse.example: A longer contact time gives a larger impulse for the same force.

terminalVelocity.name: Terminal velocity
terminalVelocity.definition: Constant speed reached when air resistance equals weight.
terminalVelocity.example: A skydiver reaches about 53 m/s in free fall.

reactionTime.name: Reaction time
reactionTime.definition: Time taken to respond to a stimulus.
reactionTime.example: A typical reaction time is 0.2–0.9 s.

brakingDistance.name: Braking distance
brakingDistance.definition: Distance travelled after brakes are applied.
brakingDistance.example: Increases with speed squared, longer on wet roads.

formulas.accelerationCaption: Acceleration: change in velocity over time

sim1.title: Newton's Second Law (F = ma)
sim1.desc: Adjust mass and applied force to see how acceleration changes.

sim2.title: Terminal Velocity
sim2.desc: Drop a skydiver and watch weight and air resistance balance out.

sim3.title: Momentum Collision
sim3.desc: Set two masses moving toward each other, then choose elastic or inelastic.

sim4.title: Reaction & Braking Distance
sim4.desc: Explore how speed, reaction time and road conditions change stopping distance.

simVectors.title: Vector Addition
simVectors.desc: Combine two forces tip-to-tail and read the resultant — magnitude and direction.

simGraphs.title: Motion Graphs (s-t, v-t, a-t)
simGraphs.desc: Switch between scenarios and watch all three motion graphs update together.
simGraphs.explainConstantV: Constant velocity: flat v-t line, zero a-t.
simGraphs.explainConstantA: Uniform acceleration: straight line v-t, flat a-t.
simGraphs.explainDecel: Uniform deceleration: v-t decreases linearly until rest, then stays zero.
simGraphs.distanceNote: Distance = area under the v-t graph.

forcesEnergy.title: Forces and Energy
forcesEnergy.intro: How forces stretch, push, lift and do work — and how pressure and density shape what floats, sinks, or holds together.

contactForce.name: Contact force
contactForce.definition: A force acting when objects touch.

elasticity.name: Elasticity
elasticity.definition: Ability to return to original shape after stretching.

hookesLaw.name: Hooke's Law
hookesLaw.definition: Extension is proportional to force applied (F = k × x).
hookesLaw.example: Doubling the force doubles the extension — until the limit.

workDone.name: Work done
workDone.definition: Energy transferred when a force moves an object (W = F × d).
workDone.example: Lifting a 10 N bag by 2 m does 20 J of work.

energyTransfer.name: Energy transfer
energyTransfer.definition: Movement of energy between objects or systems.
energyTransfer.example: Chemical energy in fuel → thermal energy in the engine.

pressure.name: Pressure
pressure.definition: Force acting per unit area (P = F / A).
pressure.example: A sharp knife focuses force on a small area, increasing pressure.

density.name: Density
density.definition: Mass per unit volume (ρ = m / V).
density.example: Water is 1000 kg/m³; iron is 7870 kg/m³.

fluidPressure.name: Fluid pressure
fluidPressure.definition: Pressure exerted by liquids or gases.

sim1.title: Hooke's Law
sim1.desc: Stretch a spring and watch the linear F-x relationship until the elastic limit.
sim1.warning: Elastic limit exceeded — spring is permanently deformed.

sim2.title: Pressure (P = F / A)
sim2.desc: See how spreading the same force across different areas changes pressure.

sim3.title: Density & Floating
sim3.desc: Compare object density with fluid density to predict floating or sinking.

sim4.title: Fluid Pressure with Depth
sim4.desc: Sink a probe into a fluid and watch hydrostatic pressure grow.

simEnergy.title: Energy Transformations (Pendulum)
simEnergy.desc: Watch potential and kinetic energy trade places as the pendulum swings.

simWorkPower.title: Work and Power
simWorkPower.desc: Lift a mass to a height in a chosen time — see the work done and power required.

electricity.title: Electricity and Magnetism
electricity.intro: Charge, current, resistance and circuits — plus the sensors and safety devices that put them to use.

current.name: Current
current.definition: Flow of electric charge (I, in amperes).
current.example: Measured with an ammeter in series.

voltage.name: Voltage
voltage.definition: Energy transferred per unit charge (V, in volts).
voltage.example: Measured with a voltmeter in parallel.

resistance.name: Resistance
resistance.definition: Opposition to current flow (R, in ohms).
resistance.example: Long thin wires have higher resistance.

charge.name: Charge
charge.definition: Property causing electrical attraction or repulsion.
charge.example: Like charges repel, opposite charges attract.

parallelCircuit.name: Parallel circuit
parallelCircuit.example: Same voltage on each branch; current splits.

ohmicConductor.name: Ohmic conductor
ohmicConductor.example: Straight V–I graph through the origin.

ldr.name: LDR
ldr.example: Resistance falls as light increases — used in streetlights.

staticElectricity.name: Static electricity
staticElectricity.example: Hair sticking to a balloon after rubbing.

fuse.name: Fuse
fuse.definition: Device that melts if current is too high.

circuitBreaker.name: Circuit breaker
circuitBreaker.definition: Safety switch that cuts off current automatically.
circuitBreaker.example: Can be reset, unlike a fuse.

efficiency.name: Efficiency
efficiency.definition: Ratio of useful output to total input.

kwh.name: Kilowatt-hour (kWh)
kwh.definition: Unit used to measure electricity usage.
kwh.example: 1 kWh = a 1000 W appliance running for 1 hour.

sim1.title: Ohm's Law (V = IR)
sim1.desc: Set voltage and resistance; current and power update live.

sim2.title: Circuit Builder — Series vs Parallel
sim2.desc: Compare bulbs in series and parallel. Toggle each bulb on or off.

sim3.title: Thermistor & LDR
sim3.desc: See how a thermistor responds to temperature and an LDR responds to light.

sim4.title: Power Consumption Calculator
sim4.desc: Estimate monthly energy and cost for everyday devices.

simIV.title: I-V Characteristics — Ohmic vs Non-ohmic
simIV.desc: Compare how current depends on voltage for a resistor, a filament lamp and a diode.

simMagnetic.title: Magnetic Fields
simMagnetic.desc: Visualise field lines and compass directions for a bar magnet, a current-carrying wire or a solenoid.

wavesOptics.title: Waves and Optics
wavesOptics.intro: From ripples on a pond to gamma rays — all waves share a few simple properties.

amplitude.name: Amplitude
amplitude.example: Higher amplitude = louder sound, brighter light.

wavelength.name: Wavelength
wavelength.definition: Distance between two matching points on waves (λ).

frequency.name: Frequency
frequency.definition: Number of waves passing per second (f, in Hz).
frequency.example: Audible sound is about 20 Hz–20 kHz.

emSpectrum.name: Electromagnetic spectrum
emSpectrum.example: Radio, micro, IR, visible, UV, X-ray, gamma.

reflection.name: Reflection
reflection.definition: Bouncing of waves from a surface.
reflection.example: Angle of incidence = angle of reflection.

refraction.name: Refraction
refraction.definition: Bending of waves entering another medium.
refraction.example: A straw looks bent in a glass of water.

diffraction.name: Diffraction
diffraction.definition: Spreading of waves through gaps or around obstacles.

filter.name: Filter
filter.example: A red filter blocks all except red light.

formulas.snell: n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂

sim1.title: Wave Explorer
sim1.desc: Tune amplitude, wavelength and frequency. Switch transverse or longitudinal.

sim2.title: Reflection & Refraction
sim2.desc: Shine a ray at a boundary and watch Snell's law in action.

sim3.title: Diffraction
sim3.desc: Pass a plane wave through a gap and watch it spread.

sim4.title: Electromagnetic Spectrum
sim4.desc: Slide along the spectrum to explore each region.

sim5.title: Converging Lens
sim5.desc: Move the object and watch the image form through three principal rays.

simSound.title: Sound Wave — Pitch & Loudness
simSound.desc: Tune frequency (pitch) and amplitude (loudness) and see the wave + air-particle compressions.

simFilters.title: Colour & Filters
simFilters.desc: Shine a coloured light through a filter onto an object and predict what you see.

criteria.title: MYP Criteria
criteria.intro: Four criteria are assessed in MYP Physics. Use the checklists to make sure your response covers everything.

A.title: Criterion A — Knowing & Understanding
A.subtitle: Recall and apply scientific knowledge (the written exam)
items: Recall scientific facts, terms and definitions accurately — vocabulary like 'momentum', 'resultant force', 'refraction' must be used precisely.
Apply scientific knowledge to solve problems in familiar situations (e.g. calculate F = ma when force and mass are given).
Apply scientific knowledge to solve problems in unfamiliar situations (transfer the same idea to a new context, e.g. why an airbag works).
Analyse and evaluate scientific information to make scientifically supported judgements.
Show your working in calculations: formula → substitution → answer → units.
Use the correct command term (state, describe, explain, compare, evaluate, calculate) when answering.

B.title: Criterion B — Inquiring & Designing
B.subtitle: Planning a lab
items: Clear research question with independent, dependent and controlled variables.
Hypothesis with scientific reasoning ('If… then… because…').
Materials and apparatus list with sizes / quantities.
Numbered method that another student could repeat exactly.
Safety considerations identified.
Plan for collecting enough data (≥ 5 values, ≥ 3 repeats).

C.title: Criterion C — Processing & Evaluating
C.subtitle: Analyzing a lab
items: Data table with units in headers and consistent decimal places.
Averages, ranges and uncertainties calculated.
Graph: title, labelled axes, suitable scale, line of best fit.
Trend described in words and linked to the science.
Conclusion answers the research question with evidence.
Evaluation: strengths, weaknesses and realistic improvements.

D.title: Criterion D — Reflecting on the Impacts of Science
D.subtitle: The essay
items: Clear scientific explanation of the issue.
Discussion of social, economic and environmental impacts.
Multiple perspectives considered (for / against / neutral).
Evidence from at least two reliable sources.
Personal opinion with justification.
References listed in a consistent format.

resources.title: Resources
resources.intro: External revision sites and references — keep them open while you study.

bbc.title: BBC Bitesize — KS3 / GCSE Physics
bbc.desc: Short topic notes and quick quizzes.

pmt.title: Physics & Maths Tutor (PMT)
pmt.desc: Past paper questions sorted by topic.

seneca.title: Seneca Learning
seneca.desc: Free interactive courses with progress tracking.

isaac.title: Isaac Physics
isaac.desc: Problem sets curated by physicists at the University of Cambridge.

practice.intro: MYP exam-style questions on this topic. Try answering first using the command term, then reveal the model answer.

footer.noTracking: No accounts, no tracking — your progress lives only in this browser.
